elements.text: Remark: The second solution amounts to the fact that g, being a polynomial with rational coefficients, is continuous for the 2-adic and 5-adic topologies on Q. By contrast, the first solution uses the "o-adic" topology, i.e., the usual real topology.
A-5 In all solutions, let G be a finite group of order m.

First solution: By Lagrange's theorem, if m is not divisible by p, then n = 0. Otherwise, let S be the set of p-tuples (ao,..., ap-1) € GP such that ao ...Ap-1 = e; then S has cardinality m?-!, which is divisible by p. Note that this set is invariant under cyclic permutation, that is, if (ao,..., ap-1) ES, then (a1,..,Ap-1,00) ES also. The fixed points under this operation are the tuples (a,...,a) with aP = e; all other tuples can be grouped into orbits under cyclic permutation, each of which has size p. Consequently, the number of a € G with aP = e is divisible by p; since that number is n + 1 (only e has order 1), this proves the claim.

Now set A3 = 1 and An = An-1 + 2n - 3 for n ≥ 4; we will prove by induction on n that F has at most A, triangles. For n = 3, since a1 + a2 + a3 = 6, a1 =a2 = a3 = 2 and hence I consists of just one triangle.

Next assume that an admissible triangulation of an (n - 1)-gon has at most An-1 triangles, and let I be an admissible triangulation of an n-gon. If any a; = 2, then we can remove the triangle of I containing vertex Vi to obtain an admissible triangulation of an (n - 1)gon; then the number of triangles in I is at most An-1 + 1 < An by induction. Otherwise, all ai ≥ 3. Now the average of al,...,An is less than 4, and thus there are more aj = 3 than a; ≥ 5. It follows that there is a sequence of k consecutive vertices in P whose degrees are 3,4,4,. .., 4,3 in order, for some k with 2 ≤ kIn- 1 (possibly k = 2, in which case there are no degree 4 vertices separating the degree 3 vertices). If we remove from I the 2k - 1 triangles which contain at least one of these vertices, then we are left with an admissible triangulation of an (n - 1)-gon. It follows that there are at most An-1 + 2k - 1 ≤ An-1+2n-3 = An triangles in T. This completes the induction step and the proof.

Second solution: (by Anand Deopurkar) Assume that n > 0, and let H be any subgroup of G of order p. Let S be the set of all elements of G H of order dividing p, and let H act on G by conjugation. Each orbit has size p except for those which consist of individual elements g which commute with H. For each such g, g and H generate an elementary abelian subgroup of G of order p?. However, we can group these g into sets of size p? - p based on which subgroup they generate together with H. Hence the cardinality of S is divisible by p; adding the p - 1 nontrivial elements of H gives n = -1 (mod p) as desired.

Third solution: Let S be the set of elements in G having order dividing p, and let H be an elementary abelian p-group of maximal order in G. If |H| = 1, then we are done. So assume |H| = pk for some k ≥ 1, and let H act on S by conjugation. Let T C S denote the set of fixed points of this action. Then the size of every Horbit on S divides pk, and so |S| = |T| (mod p). On the other hand, H C T, and if T contained an element not in H, then that would contradict the maximality of H. It follows that H = T, and so |S| = |T | = |H| = pk = 0 (mod p), i.e., (S| = n + 1 is a multiple of p.

Remark: We can refine the bound An somewhat. Supposing that ai ≥ 3 for all i, the fact that a1 + ...+ an ≤ 4n - 6 implies that there are at least six more indices i with aj = 3 than with ai ≥ 5. Thus there exist six sequences with degrees 3,4,..., 4,3, of total length at most n + 6. We may thus choose a sequence of length k ≤ LöJ + 1, so we may improve the upper bound to An = An-1 + 2L6| + 1, or asymptotically on.
However (as noted by Noam Elkies), a hexagonal swatch of a triangular lattice, with the boundary as close to regular as possible, achieves asymptotically in triangles.

Remark: This result is a theorem of Cauchy; the first solution above is due to McKay. A more general (and more difficult) result was proved by Frobenius: for any positive integer m, if G is a finite group of order divisible by m, then the number of elements of G of order dividing m is a multiple of m.
B-1 The problem fails if f is allowed to be constant, e.g., take f(n) = 1. We thus assume that f is nonconstant. Write f(n) = El-o ain' with ai > 0. Then
A-6 For an admissible triangulation T, number the vertices of P consecutively v1,..., Vn, and let aj be the number of edges in I emanating from vi; note that aj ≥ 2 for all i.

We first claim that a1 +...+ an < 4n -6. Let V,E,F denote the number of vertices, edges, and faces in I. By Euler's Formula, (F + 1) - E + V = 2 (one must add 1 to the face count for the region exterior to P). Each face has three edges, and each edge but the n outside edges belongs to two faces; hence F = 2E - n. On the other hand, each edge has two endpoints, and each of the V - n internal vertices is an endpoint of at least 6 edges; hence a1 + ... + an + 6(V -n) ≤ 2E. Combining this inequality with the previous two equations gives
If n = 1, then this implies that f (f(n) + 1) is divisible by f(n). Otherwise, 0 < f(1) < f(n) since f is nonconstant and has positive coefficients, so f(f(n) + 1) cannot be divisible by f(n).
